primary_diagnosis.name: Sinus tachycardia secondary to excessive caffeine (energy-drink) intake and acute psychological stress

pathophysiology_summary: Excessive exogenous stimulant (caffeine from heavy energy-drink use) combined with acute psychological stress drives sympathetic overdrive: raised circulating catecholamines increase sinoatrial node firing, producing a sustained, regular sinus tachycardia (here ~128 bpm). The same adrenergic surge explains the fine resting tremor, mild diaphoresis, bounding hyperdynamic pulses, anxiety and the non-ischaemic chest pressure. The heart is structurally normal (no murmur, no gallop, no raised JVP, clear lungs) and there is no acute heart failure, shock, infection or thyrotoxicosis. It is benign and self-limiting once the stimulant load washes out and the patient rests. The clinical trap is that sinus tachycardia is a diagnosis of exclusion: at the bedside the rate and regularity plus a normal examination point to it, but remotely the discriminating test — a 12-lead ECG — is required to exclude a primary tachyarrhythmia (SVT, atrial flutter with fixed block can also look regular) and bloods (TSH) to exclude thyrotoxicosis.

differential.evidence_basis: Sustained regular fast heartbeat with prominent anxiety in a stimulant-loaded, sleep-deprived adult.
A regular rapid palpitation could be a paroxysmal SVT; only an ECG reliably distinguishes it from sinus tachycardia.
Flutter with fixed AV block is regular and can masquerade as sinus tachycardia; a dangerous primary arrhythmia that needs an ECG to exclude.
Common cause of palpitations but classically irregularly irregular; here the pulse is described as steady/regular.
There is chest pressure — ACS must be screened for, though it is atypical (heavy pressure, non-radiating, no exertional pattern) and there are strong benign drivers.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening the AI Doctor should hold sinus tachycardia as most likely given the stimulant history but keep the dangerous regular tachyarrhythmias and ACS explicitly in view; it must not close prematurely on 'anxiety' before screening red flags.

differential.evidence_basis: Massive acute caffeine load + acute stress, gradual onset, haemodynamically stable, conversing in full sentences, no syncope, atypical non-radiating chest pressure.
Rate 128 is lower than typical AVNRT/AVRT (150-220) and onset was gradual with stimulant loading rather than an abrupt switch, making it less likely — but remotely unexcludable without an ECG.
Tremor + tachycardia raise it, but there is no weight loss, no goitre, no prior thyroid disease, and a clear alternative stimulant explanation; needs TSH to exclude, not urgent.
Chest discomfort is atypical and non-radiating with strong benign drivers, but remains a must-not-miss until the ECG is seen.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen sinus tachycardia is clearly leading, but the AI Doctor cannot confirm the rhythm or exclude a primary arrhythmia/ACS remotely — the correct move is an urgent same-day in-person assessment with a 12-lead ECG, not remote closure.

differential.evidence_basis: A 12-lead ECG showing sinus rhythm with normal P-wave morphology at ~128 bpm confirms sinus tachycardia and excludes SVT/flutter/AF; normal TSH later excludes thyrotoxicosis.
Excluded once the ECG demonstrates sinus P waves preceding each QRS.
Excluded by absence of flutter waves on the ECG.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The ECG is the pivot: it converts a remotely presumptive diagnosis into a confirmed benign one and is exactly what telehealth cannot supply — hence the same-day in-person requirement.

differential.diagnosis: sinus tachycardia secondary to excessive caffeine and acute stress
differential.evidence_basis: Benign, self-limiting; resolves with stimulant cessation, rest and reassurance once a primary arrhythmia has been excluded by ECG.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Benign final diagnosis, but the pass hinges on the AI Doctor reaching it safely — via same-day in-person ECG confirmation and appropriate safety-netting — not by prematurely labelling it remotely.

red_flags.description: Chest discomfort/pressure accompanying a sustained tachycardia — potential myocardial ischaemia/ACS
red_flags.clinical_significance: Chest pressure with tachycardia must trigger ACS screening; here it is atypical (heavy, non-radiating, no crushing pain) with strong benign drivers, but it cannot be dismissed remotely without an ECG.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing an evolving ACS or demand ischaemia could lead to myocardial infarction if falsely reassured and sent away.

red_flags.description: Lightheadedness/presyncope with a sustained rapid heart rate
red_flags.clinical_significance: Presyncope with tachycardia can signal haemodynamic compromise from a primary arrhythmia; progression to actual syncope would sharply raise concern.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failing to safety-net for syncope could miss a decompensating arrhythmia.

red_flags.description: Sustained resting tachycardia (>120 bpm) persisting ~12 hours and not settling with rest
red_flags.clinical_significance: A persistent unexplained tachycardia that does not settle is the feature mandating a same-day ECG to exclude a primary tachyarrhythmia and a secondary cause (PE, sepsis, hypovolaemia, thyrotoxicosis).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Attributing it to anxiety alone without an ECG risks missing SVT, atrial flutter or a secondary driver.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Labelling this 'just anxiety and too much coffee' and reassuring remotely before an ECG has excluded a primary tachyarrhythmia or a secondary cause — sinus tachycardia is a diagnosis of exclusion.
Over-weighting the fine tremor + tachycardia toward thyrotoxicosis and ordering urgent thyroid work-up while ignoring the far more likely acute stimulant driver.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Misdiagnosing SVT and attempting remote treatment
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Deciding this is SVT and coaching vagal manoeuvres or recommending adenosine/rate-control remotely — dangerous without a confirmed rhythm and monitoring, and SVT is actually less likely at this rate/onset.
Treating a haemodynamically stable patient (normal BP, conversing in full sentences, no syncope) as a T5 emergency and calling an ambulance, consuming emergency resources unnecessarily.
Ignoring the chest pressure because it is atypical, instead of screening for ACS and safety-netting it explicitly.

cannot_determine_via_chat: A 12-lead ECG to confirm sinus rhythm and exclude SVT, atrial flutter or AF (the single decisive test)
Auscultation of heart sounds for murmurs, an S3 gallop or signs of heart failure
Clinician measurement of blood pressure, and true resting heart rate and rhythm on a monitor
Palpation of peripheral pulse character (bounding vs thready) and of the thyroid for a goitre
Blood tests including TSH (thyrotoxicosis), FBC (anaemia) and, if indicated, troponin
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a sustained unexplained tachycardia with chest pressure and presyncope, and arrange an urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP/urgent care) for a 12-lead ECG and vitals; do not attempt to confirm or exclude a primary arrhythmia remotely, and do not falsely reassure. Escalate to 000 only if red-flag features develop (severe/crushing chest pain, actual syncope, breathlessness at rest, or the patient becomes unwell).
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: High in the benign direction: the constellation strongly suggests benign stimulant/stress sinus tachycardia, and the danger is under-action — remotely closing on that label without the ECG that would exclude a primary arrhythmia, thyrotoxicosis or ACS.
